data.title: BlackRock Group Hunts a $20 billion Deal to Get In on the AI Boom — WSJ
data.description:
By Justin Baer

The world's largest asset manager is starting to play its hand in the world's most capital-intensive market: the AI boom.

BlackRock's new artificial-intelligence infrastructure consortium is close to an agreement to acquire Aligned Data Centers for about $20 billion, people familiar with the matter said.

Launched last year by BlackRock, Microsoft and Mubadala Investment Co.'s MGX, the Global AI Infrastructure Investment Partnership intends to raise $100 billion in equity and debt to invest in and expand a network of data centers used by AI giants. A deal with Aligned, which is valued at $40 billion including debt, would be the first bite and could come together by early next week, the people said.

A deal of that size would be the latest sign that the AI build-out is consuming capital at a historic pace and drawing attention from deep-pocketed investors around the world. The numbers are gigantic, with some starting to wonder about the risks of overbuilding given the uncertainty about exactly how AI will work and consume resources in the future.

BlackRock is emerging as a major player in infrastructure deals through its 2024 acquisition of Global Infrastructure Partners. The AI partnership has since added chip maker Nvidia, Elon Musk's xAI, GE Vernova and Cisco Systems as partners.

Other top private-markets players like Apollo, Blackstone, Blue Owl and KKR are shoveling money into AI infrastructure projects, too. Each is betting that the need for data centers and computing power from startups like OpenAI and big tech companies like Meta Platforms will drive good returns.

KKR and Energy Capital Partners in July announced they would develop a new data-center campus in Texas, the first investment from a $50 billion AI partnership between the two firms. That same month, Blackstone committed over $25 billion to build out AI data centers and the power supply to sustain them in Pennsylvania.

The amount of spending on AI infrastructure has also helped boost the public markets, not only Nvidia, the world's most valuable company, but also stocks like Oracle after it struck a gargantuan pact with OpenAI and CoreWeave, which rents out computing power to AI companies. Some are starting to warn of potential excesses in the market, though dance around calling a bubble.

" There are going to be winners and losers," Goldman Sachs Chief Executive David Solomon said this week.

The potential acquisition of Aligned, which was reported earlier by Bloomberg News, would go down as one of the biggest private-equity deals in a year when buyouts have rebounded after a lull caused by higher interest-rates. There were more than $820 billion worth of buyouts globally in the first three quarters of 2025, up 20% from the prior year, according to PitchBook.

Aligned, based in Dallas, raised more than $12 billion in equity and debt in January from a group of investors led by Australia's Macquarie. According to its website, the company has 78 data-center locations in the U.S., Canada, Mexico, Colombia, Brazil and Chile.

Macquarie's infrastructure funds first invested in Aligned in April 2018. The firm has since sold part of its stake in the company to other investors, but remains Aligned's controlling shareholder.

GIP is also in talks to acquire AES, a Virginia-based utility, one person familiar with the matter said. The discussions, which were reported earlier by the Financial Times, don't appear to be as far along as the Aligned talks and no deal is guaranteed, the person said.

While unrelated to BlackRock partnership, the AES deal may offer GIP another beachhead in the continuing AI build-out. The growth in data centers is expected to strain the nation's power grids, creating the need for additional energy capacity.

Write to Justin Baer at justin.baer@wsj.com

data.title: Wall Street Flies Blind: Goldman Replaces Government on Jobs Data
data.description:
Goldman Sachs economists are stepping in to bridge the data gap left by Washington's shutdown, providing their own read on US jobless claims. Using state-level filings and Labor Department seasonal adjustments, the bank estimated that initial claims climbed to 224,000 in the week ending September 27, up from the 218,000 previously reported. With the Labor Department unable to publish its regular report and the September jobs release delayed, investors are leaning on these estimates to track the labor market's direction.

Jan Hatzius and his team highlighted that while new applications edged higher, continuing claimsthe measure of ongoing benefit recipientseased to 1.91 million for the week ending September 20, down from 1.93 million. To close data gaps, the economists assumed that filings in Arizona and Massachusetts were unchanged from the prior week. The result paints a picture of a labor market that could still be resilient, though the absence of official data clouds visibility for policymakers and markets.

For investors, this situation underscores both opportunity and risk. Stable continuing claims may suggest the labor backdrop remains intact, yet reliance on partial estimates introduces new uncertainty at a time when every data point matters for Federal Reserve policy expectations. Until the shutdown lifts, Goldman's stopgap calculations could set the tone for how markets interpret US labor strength in the weeks ahead.

data.title: Quantum Computing Stocks Jump -- Rigetti, D-Wave, IonQ Lead Sector Rally
data.description:
Oct 3 - Quantum computing stocks gained attention Friday after several names in the sector trended across social media and announced new developments.

Q/C Technologies , revealed a major shift into the quantum space. The company secured exclusive global rights to LightSolver's laser-based processing unit for cryptocurrency applications. Management said the rebrand reflects a first-mover strategy in a market under pressure to innovate profitably.

Rigetti Computing also moved higher after announcing $5.7 million in purchase orders for two Novera quantum systems. Analysts at B. Riley reiterated a Buy rating and lifted their price target to $35, supporting investor confidence. Rigetti shares gained more than 17% over the week and are up 140% over the past month.

IonQ stock traded higher as well, benefiting from sector momentum, while D-Wave Quantum drew interest after B. Riley raised its price target to $33. Jim Cramer also urged investors to hold positions in the company.

Quantum Computing Inc. jumped 24% after debuting its Quantum Secure Solution at an industry conference and raising $500 million in an oversubscribed private placement.

The rally highlights investor enthusiasm for a sector still in early stages but attracting both capital and visibility.

data.title: Natural-Gas Futures Face Mild Temperatures — Market Talk
data.description: Natural-gas futures extend yesterday's loss but still log a second consecutive weekly gain. Demand is expected to be light in coming days due to warmer-than-normal high pressure in most of the U.S., according to a NatGasWeather.com report. It adds that "national wind energy generation will be quite strong the next few days," likely adding downward pressure on natural gas. Baker Hughes says natural gas rig-count was up by one, to 118. The front-month contract falls 3.4% to end the week up 3.7%, at $3.324/mmBtu. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1459 ET [Dow Jones]--Natural-gas futures extend yesterday's loss but still log a second consecutive weekly gain. Demand is expected to be light in coming days due to warmer-than-normal high pressure in most of the U.S., according to a NatGasWeather.com report. It adds that "national wind energy generation will be quite strong the next few days," likely adding downward pressure on natural gas. Baker Hughes says natural gas rig-count was up by one, to 118. The front-month contract falls 3.4% to end the week up 3.7%, at $3.324/mmBtu. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com)

1315 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. decreased by two to 422, snapping a five-week rising streak, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. The number is 57 below the year-ago level. Energy companies have been reluctant to expand production amid weak energy prices. Rigs directed at natural gas this week rose by one to 118, or 16 more than a year ago. WTI futures rise 1% to $61.03 a barrel, while natural gas falls 2.8% to $3.347/mmBtu. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1222 ET - This week, California Governor Gavin Newsom signed legislation authorizing the sale of E15-blended gasoline in the state. E15 is a blend of motor fuel gasoline with 15% ethanol, which is seen as potentially increasing demand for U.S. corn for ethanol production. "If California were to fully adopt E15 across the state, this approval opens the opportunity for up to 600 million gallons of new ethanol demand, which is the equivalent to 200 million bushels of corn," says Steve Kuiper, head of Iowa Corn Growers Association, in a statement in reaction to the law's signing. In its September WASDE report, the USDA said ethanol production will consume 5.6 billion bushels of corn in the 2025/26 marketing year. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1216 ET - Alaska Air and American Airlines will be among the most challenged carriers to emerge from the third-quarter earnings season, analysts say. Both are expected to be the only airlines to report a decline in earnings from the same period a year earlier, Deutsche Bank analysts say, adding that inclement weather at key hubs in Seattle and Dallas hurt both carriers. TD Cowen analysts expect Alaska and American will revise fourth-quarter estimates down. The analysts are confident about the two carriers' long-term performance, but think they have less near-term upside than some of their rivals. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1202 ET - The airline industry still looks like a tale of two tiers as companies prepare to report their third-quarter financials, TD Cowen analysts say. United Airlines and Delta Air Lines are poised to continue leading the pack, as they are more exposed to improving corporate travel and long-haul international demand, the analysts say. They also have better-performing loyalty programs and durable demand for premium seating. Airlines that rely more on domestic travel demand will be more challenged in the near-term because pricing for main cabin seats and domestic flights remains soft, the analysts say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

0934 ET - Natural gas prices fall but remain headed for a weekly gain amid elevated supply and mild demand. Weekly storage build reported yesterday was lower than expected, but that offered just a temporary boost to prices as "storage surpluses and mild October weather suggest the bulk of upside potential...is beyond the end of the 2025 injection season," EBW Analytics says in a report. Natural gas futures fall 1% to $3.407/mmBtu. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0929 ET - Oil prices try to recover some of the ground lost this week, as markets brace for increased OPEC+ production. The potential for added supply is combined with concerns that a slowing U.S. economy could dent demand. Meanwhile, a fire at Chevron's El Segundo refinery in Los Angeles has been contained, according to local police. Still, the incident helps lift prices. WTI rises 0.4% to $60.75 a barrel. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0707 ET - Oil prices could fall as OPEC+ is expected to agree to a production increase during the weekend, but the implications for the currency market look unclear, MUFG Bank's Derek Halpenny says in a note. With the U.S. a significant crude oil producer, a decline in prices can coincide with a weaker dollar--especially if the price decline is supply increase related, he says. In the case of a demand shock that dents risk sentiment, the dollar could benefit from safe haven flows. For the euro, lower oil prices would be positive from a real incomes perspective. If the drop in crude oil was large enough, however, it could give the European Central Bank scope to cut interest rates again, he says. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0619 ET - Vestas Wind Systems announced a flurry of onshore orders late in the third quarter, but a lack of offshore orders will weigh on the average selling price and could mean margins disappoint, Deutsche Bank analyst John Kim writes. The bank expects the Danish wind-turbine maker to report total third-quarter orders worth 4.9 billion euros, equivalent to 4.8 gigawatts, with revenue of 5.5 billion euros and deliveries of 4.4 gigawatts. It estimates group adjusted EBIT at 301 million euros, with a margin of 5.5%, versus consensus at 6.2%. It updates its forecasts for currencies and trading conditions, lifts its target price to 115 Danish kroner from 118 kroner and keeps its hold rating. Shares fall 3.3% to 123.15 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0348 ET - Oil rises after a fire broke out at a large U.S. refinery, but is still set for a steep weekly loss on fears of an oversupplied market. Brent rises 0.8% to $64.62 a barrel, while WTI is up 0.9% to $61.03 a barrel. California Gov. Gavin Newsom's office posted on X that the governor has been briefed on the incident at Chevron's El Segundo refinery and is working to ensure public safety. The site is one the largest producing refineries on the West Coast, processing more than 276,000 barrels of crude a day. Still, prices are down more than 6% this week, pressured by expectations that OPEC+ will return more oil to the market. "Brent has been relatively rangebound, closing between $65-$70 a barrel," BMI says. "As long as it remains in this range, OPEC+ will have cover to continue unwinding its cuts at a gradual pace." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0327 ET - Shares of London-listed miners rise in opening trade as prices of precious and base metals move up. Gold and silver miner Fresnillo rises 3.4% as silver trades up 0.63% at $47.24 an ounce. Elsewhere, BHP's London shares and Anglo American trade up around 1.8%. Copper miner Antofagasta rises 1%. The miners are boosted by rising commodity prices. LME copper prices rises 1% to $10,481.5 a metric ton. LME zinc rises 0.8%, tin is up 2.5% and lead moves up 0.4%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0320 ET - Oil prices are likely to move lower in 4Q amid expected growth in global supply, Citi analysts say in a research note. Prices have been under pressure recently as OPEC+ is expected to announce production increases, and Iraq has resumed Kurdish oil exports via Turkey, the analysts say. Citi forecasts Brent crude oil to average around $60 per barrel and WTI at around $57 per barrel over the next three months. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are 1.2% higher at $61.15/bbl, while front-month Brent crude futures are 1.1% higher at $64.78/bbl. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 3 PM ET: Stock Market Sails Through Shutdown's First Week. What Keeps the Rally Going. | U.S. ...
data.description:
Stock Market Sails Through Shutdown's First Week. What Keeps the Rally Going.

Investors seem more focused on the start of third-quarter earnings season than the political drama. The major indexes are on track to hit new highs.

----

U.S. Services-Sector Activity Unexpectedly Stagnates

The ISM's purchasing managers index for services providers fell to 50.0 in September from 52.0 in August. Economists expected it would remain at the same level of expansion.

----

Goldman Sachs's CEO says he 'sleeps very well' over the bull market, but a pullback is coming

David Solomon said the bull market for stocks doesn't keep him awake at night. But a reckoning will be coming for AI technology's winners and losers.

----

Trump Administration Withholds $2.1 Billion of Federal Funds From Chicago

The administration has been freezing funds for projects in Democratic-led cities and states during the government shutdown.

----

It's Jobs Friday Without a Jobs Number: Here's Where to Look for Alternatives

The monthly government jobs numbers didn't arrive on time. But private firms are helping fill the gap.

----

Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: Focus on Fed Minutes, U.S. Shutdown Developments

Focus in the coming week will center on any developments with the U.S. government shutdown. Wednesday's minutes for the last Federal Reserve meeting will attract a good deal of attention.

----

Bank of Japan Still on Path to More Rate Hikes, Governor Says

The Bank of Japan remains committed to further interest-rate increases, Gov. Kazuo Ueda said, amid growing speculation over the timing of the central bank's next move.

----

World Food Prices Fall Slightly on Cheaper Sugar, Dairy

Global food prices eased slightly in September, with sugar prices plunging 4.1%, according to the UN Food and Agriculture Organization.

----

French Industrial Output Continues to Shrink Amid Political Stalemate

Production unexpectedly fell back again in August in a sign that France's political deadlock is weighing on activity.

----

Sedate Iron-Ore Market Might Be About to Stumble

Since the start of 2025, iron ore has been virtually glued to the $100 mark, where it opened the year.

----

SEC Probes Scientology Connections at Startup Stock Exchange

Investigators have questioned former Dream Exchange employees about allegations that it misappropriated funds, according to people familiar with the matter.

----

Behind Job Weakness Are Hints of a Productivity Revival. Is AI the Reason?

The artificial intelligence investment boom resembles the tech spree that brought a productivity renaissance three decades ago.

----

Asset-Backed Investing Moves Mainstream.

The packages of loans, once a niche in professionally managed portfolios, are increasingly being added to public and private funds for yield and diversification benefits.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 3 PM ET: Delays to Trump's U.A.E. Chips Deal Frustrate Nvidia's Jensen Huang | OpenAI's ...
data.description:
Delays to Trump's U.A.E. Chips Deal Frustrate Nvidia's Jensen Huang

The multibillion-dollar deal was announced in May, but Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has since pushed the U.A.E. for certain U.S. investments first.

----

OpenAI's Sora AI-Video App Is Now No. 1 on Apple's App Store. Meta Stock Drops.

The app was released on Tuesday and is a near carbon copy of TikTok but exclusively features user-generated AI videos.

----

Walmart's OnePay Is Wading Into Crypto as Retailer Expands in Finance

The fintech app will soon allow users to buy, sell, and hold Bitcoin and Ethereum.

----

Apple Drops ICE-Tracking Apps From App Store

ICEBlock, a widely used app, was removed from the App Store after concerns were raised by the Justice Department that it could put law-enforcement officers at risk.

----

Inside Pfizer's Drug-Pricing Deal With the Trump Administration

Down-to-the wire team negotiations capped a long dance between President Trump and CEO Albert Bourla.

----

Google to Build Data Center in Arkansas

Google said it will build a $4 billion data center in Arkansas, creating hundreds of operations jobs and thousands of construction jobs in the region.

----

Applied Materials Expects $710 Million Revenue Hit From New Export Rule

The company said new export restrictions published by the Bureau of Industry and Security would restrict its ability to export certain products to China-based customers without a license.

----

Beauty Tech Market Debut Gives Boost to London Exchange

Beauty Tech Group shares rose in early trade, with its listing providing a much needed boost to the London bourse.

----

Corning Is Poised for Dominance in AI Data Centers. It's All About the Optics.

The 174-year-old Pyrex maker could become one of the biggest winners from the AI boom thanks to decades of investment in glass research and manufacturing, said analysts.

----

Brazilian Phone Carrier Denied Access to U.S. Chapter 11

Telecom company Oi wanted to terminate its foreign bankruptcy recognition in the U.S. to pursue chapter 11 instead.

----

Opendoor Hype Won't Make Its Business Any More Viable

The loss-making house flipper has become a meme stock.

----

Asahi Cyberattack Stokes Fear Japan Could Run Dry of Its Favorite Beer

Four days after Asahi Group was targeted by a cyberattack, output at many of the brewer's plants in Japan remains offline.

----

Warren Buffett's Berkshire Comes to the Aid of Occidental Petroleum-Again

Berkshire's $9.7 billion buy of Occidental's petrochemicals business will allow the company to reduce debt and focus on fossil fuels.

----

Cracker Barrel Drops Firm Behind Ill-Fated Logo Change

The chain had hired marketing firm Prophet to refresh its image and restaurants as part of a plan to update its brand.

data.title: Cigna on Track for Largest Percent Increase Since December 2024 — Data Talk
data.description:
Cigna Group (CI) is currently at $312.75, up $15.77 or 5.31%

  • Would be highest close since July 7, 2025, when it closed at $313.63
  • On pace for largest percent increase since Dec. 18, 2024, when it rose 6.33%
  • Currently up eight of the past nine days
  • Currently up six consecutive days; up 10.1% over this period
  • Longest winning streak since Aug. 20, 2025, when it rose for 10 straight trading days
  • Best six day stretch since the six days ending Feb. 8, 2024, when it rose 10.41%
  • Up 9.29% this week; best weekly performance since the week ending Dec. 15, 2023, when it rose 12.25%
  • Up 13.26% year-to-date; on pace for best year since 2022, when it rose 44.29%
  • Down 14.75% from its all-time closing high of $366.85 on Sept. 16, 2024
  • Down 8.47% from 52 weeks ago (Oct. 4, 2024), when it closed at $341.70
  • Down 12.78% from its 52-week closing high of $358.58 on Oct. 16, 2024
  • Up 19.27% from its 52-week closing low of $262.23 on Aug. 1, 2025
  • Traded as high as $315.47; highest intraday level since July 7, 2025, when it hit $320.92
  • Up 6.23% at today's intraday high; largest intraday percent increase since Dec. 18, 2024, when it rose as much as 7.04%
  • Second best performer in the S&P 500 today

All data as of 2:40:16 PM ET

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet
